exercises.prompt: « Moi président, je… Moi président, je… » — which device?
exercises.explanation: The same opening hammered — anaphora, fifteen times in the famous 2012 debate.

exercises.prompt: « Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité » owes part of its power to…

options.text: le rythme ternaire — three feels true to French ears

exercises.explanation: The rule of three: two feels short, four feels long, three lands.

exercises.prompt: A question that is an answer.
exercises.template: « Allons-nous rester les bras croisés ? » est une question {{b1}}.
exercises.explanation: Nobody is expected to answer; everybody is expected to agree.

exercises.template: « C'est un abus, c'est une faute, c'est un crime » : une {{b1}}.
exercises.explanation: Each step harder than the last — gradation.

exercises.prompt: The C1 analysis grid for any device:
exercises.explanation: Name it, say what it does, say why the orator wants that.

exercises.template: L'oratrice ne cesse de {{b1}} le mot « justice ».
exercises.explanation: Marteler = to hammer home, the orator's percussion.

exercises.prompt: Match each device to its signature example.

pairs.right: « un abus, une faute, un crime »

exercises.prompt: Put the words in order: "Her words galvanised the crowd."
Listen to the speech fragment. Which two devices stack?
exercises.transcript: Nous avons attendu, nous avons espéré, nous avons agi.

options.text: Anaphora (nous avons…) carried by ternary rhythm (three clauses)

exercises.explanation: Three identical openings — anaphora and the rule of three working together.

exercises.prompt: Read this speech-analysis paragraph and answer both questions.
exercises.passageFr: Le discours s'ouvre sur une apostrophe — « Vous, les invisibles » — qui fait exister son public avant de le défendre. Suit une anaphore en « on nous a dit », martelée quatre fois : l'orateur installe la litanie des promesses trahies. La chute, elle, tient en trois mots — « plus jamais ça » — où le rythme ternaire transforme la colère en mot d'ordre.
exercises.passageEn: The speech opens with an apostrophe — "You, the invisible ones" — which brings its audience into existence before defending it. Then comes an anaphora on "we were told", hammered four times: the orator sets up the litany of betrayed promises. The closing line holds in three words — "never again" — where ternary rhythm turns anger into a rallying cry.

questions.prompt: What does the opening apostrophe DO (effect + intention)?

options.text: It brings the audience into existence before defending it
It apologises for the speech's length

questions.prompt: What does the anaphora « on nous a dit » install?

exercises.prompt: What no transcript shows, but every great orator uses:

options.text: le silence — the pause that lands the blow

exercises.explanation: Delivery is half the art: the pause, the hammered word, the eye on the room.

exercises.prompt: Listen, then choose what it means.
exercises.transcript: Moi présidente, je protégerai ; moi présidente, j'écouterai.

options.text: As president, I will protect; as president, I will listen.
Are we going to stand idly by?
It is an abuse, it is a fault, it is a crime.
